--- title: 5.1. PLSR vs. CNN Learning Curves keywords: fastai sidebar: home_sidebar summary: "Visualizing the behaviours (performances) of PLSR and CNN models as the number of samples increases" description: "Visualizing the behaviours (performances) of PLSR and CNN models as the number of samples increases" nb_path: "nbs/19_paper.figures.learning_curves.ipynb" ---
{% raw %}
{% endraw %} {% raw %} 
%load_ext autoreload
%autoreload 2
{% endraw %} {% raw %} 
from pathlib import Path
import pickle

from mirzai.vis.core import (centimeter, PRIMARY_COLOR, 
                             plot_learning_curve, plot_capacity,
                             set_style, DEFAULT_STYLE)

# Data vis.
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.gridspec import GridSpec

# Data science stack
import numpy as np

import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')
{% endraw %}

Input data

To generate the learning curves for both the PLSR and CNN models, run the following notebooks:

  • PLSR Learning curve
  • CNN Learning curve

Instead, we load already generated and saved data: history_pls_learning_curve.pickle and history_cnn_learning_curve.pickle.

{% raw %} 
src_dir = Path('./files/dumps')
{% endraw %} {% raw %} 
fnames = ['history_pls_learning_curve.pickle', 'history_cnn_learning_curve.pickle'] 
history_pls, history_cnn = [pickle.load(open(src_dir/fname, "rb")) for fname in fnames]
{% endraw %} {% raw %} 
(history_pls, history_cnn)

(OrderedDict([('nb_samples',
               [80, 400, 800, 2000, 4000, 8000, 16000, 24000, 32105]),
              ('train_score',

                0.04249883519900565,
                0.0434702722437448]),
              ('valid_score',
               [0.5539436385407241,
                0.762776080499277,
                1.0162277584774049,
                0.3331475794310779,
                0.08961999971737833,

               [22, 92, 172, 600, 1200, 1000, 1000, 1000, 800])]),

               [80, 400, 800, 2000, 4000, 8000, 16000, 24000, 32105]),
              ('train_score',
               [0.008633846106628576,
                0.009632993776064653,
                0.008937590755522251,

                0.009795784916728735,
                0.009933573609218002,

               [0.16574089229106903,
                0.09618065319955349,
                0.09235746094158717,
                0.060032136156223714,
                0.05089669139124453,
                0.05477035137277747,
                0.037039474181830886,
                0.030894687944190934,

              ('nb_epochs',
               [171, 251, 371, 321, 531, 1281, 1831, 3051, 2271])]))
{% endraw %}

Plot

{% raw %} 
def plot_learning_curves(history_pls, history_cnn,
                         figsize=(16*centimeter,10*centimeter), dpi=600):
    # Layout 
    fig = plt.figure(figsize=figsize, dpi=600)

    gs = GridSpec(nrows=2, ncols=2, height_ratios=[4,1])

    ax0 = fig.add_subplot(gs[0, 0])
    ax0.set_title('(a)', loc='left')

    ax1 = fig.add_subplot(gs[1, 0], sharex=ax0)
    ax1.set_title('(c)', loc='left')

    ax2 = fig.add_subplot(gs[0, 1], sharey=ax0)
    ax2.set_title('(b)', loc='left')

    ax3 = fig.add_subplot(gs[1, 1])
    ax3.set_title('(d)', loc='left')

    # Plots
    for (ax, history) in zip([ax0, ax2], [history_pls, history_cnn]):
        x, losses_train, losses_valid, _ = history.values()
        train_params = {'marker': 'o', 'mfc':'w', 'ms':3, 'c':'C0'} 
        valid_params = {'marker': 'o', 'mfc':'w', 'ms':3, 'c':'C1'} 
        plot_learning_curve(x, losses_train, losses_valid, ax=ax, 
                            train_kwargs=train_params, valid_kwargs=valid_params)

    for (ax, history) in zip([ax1, ax3], [history_pls, history_cnn]):
        x, _, _, capacity = history.values()
        plot_capacity(x, capacity, ax=ax) 
                           
    # Ornaments
    ax0.legend(loc='best', frameon=False) 
    ax0.set_ylabel('Mean Squared Error →', loc='top')
    ax1.set_ylabel('Capacity →', loc='top')
    ax1.set_xlabel('Data size →', loc='right')
    ax3.set_xlabel('Data size →', loc='right')
    for ax in [ax0, ax1, ax2, ax3]:
        ax.grid(True, "minor", color="0.85", linewidth=0.2, zorder=-20)
        ax.grid(True, "major", color="0.65", linewidth=0.4, zorder=-10) 

    plt.tight_layout()
{% endraw %} {% raw %} 
FIG_PATH = Path('nameofyourfolder')
set_style(DEFAULT_STYLE)
plot_learning_curves(history_pls, history_cnn)

# To save/export it
# plt.savefig(FIG_PATH/'learning-curves.png', dpi=600, transparent=True, format='png')
{% endraw %}
